narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was carefully evaluated by our admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam outcomes based on academic and personal profiles like yours. The model assessed your Year 1 school decile of 3, Year 3 decile of 4, undergraduate GPA of 2.20, LSAT score of 22, and your plan to attend full-time—details that paint a picture of your journey so far. It estimated a 64% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is why your application was rejected; the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, which stands well below the typical applicant's average of around 37 and strongly signaled higher risk to the model. In contrast, your upper-middle family income provided a modest positive nudge toward passing, likely reflecting resources that support many successful students. Your Year 1 decile also helped a bit by suggesting some early academic resilience compared to lower performers, while your Year 3 decile offered a smaller boost in the same vein. Planning to attend full-time aligned with the vast majority of students (over 92%) who pass, giving your profile a slight favorable tilt there, and even your undergraduate GPA had a minor neutral-to-positive lean. Overall, though, your LSAT score's dominant weight overshadowed these other elements, as it deviated sharply from what the model sees in typical admitted students who pass the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a person or your determination—many factors like grit and study habits aren't captured here—but a data-driven call to strengthen key predictors. To move forward, we recommend retaking the LSAT to aim closer to the average, bolstering your GPA through post-bac courses if possible, and highlighting any upward trends in your school deciles on future applications. You're clearly committed, and with targeted improvements, you can shift the odds in your favor for the next cycle. Keep pushing; law school doors reopen for prepared applicants like you.
